observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE
at least once a year and submit the RPCPPE classified per category/type
to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and coordinate
closely and seek assistance from the City Accounting Office for the
classification of PPE according to category and in reconciling the results
of the RPCPPE with the accountability records;
b) City Accounting Office to continue its efforts to determine the details of
the PPE amounting to P489,524,727.30; and
c) CGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein the relevant
information for proper monitoring.

We reiterated our recommendations that the City Mayor instruct the City
Treasurer and Market Supervisor to:
a. issue the new Contracts of Lease and/or facilitate the execution of formal
agreements with the market stallholders, making sure that the said
contracts are prepared in accordance with the 2015 Market Code of
General Trias;
b. send demand letters to delinquent stallholders to collect the market stall
rental fees and electricity bills totaling P5,551,704.10 plus the
corresponding surcharges, with the reminder that their failure to settle
their accounts within 30 days from due date is a sufficient ground for the
automatic cancellation of the contract of lease;
c. require all market stallholders to secure Mayor’s or business permits and
license, otherwise, impose upon them the sanctions or penalties provided
under relevant City ordinance/s;
d. investigate and explain the discrepancies between the CTO and BPLO
records, specifically on the 37 stallholders who secured business permits
from the BPLO but whose names did not appear in the record of the CTO
as stall lessees;
e. investigate and explain why seven business owners with permits were
allowed to lease two or more market stalls which were either not located
in the same section of the market or not adjacent to one another, in
violation of Section 16 of the 2015 Market Code of General Trias;
f. update and complete the records of individual market stallholders to
include relevant information such as their names, residential addresses,
contact numbers, business permits and license numbers and issuance
dates, market rental fees and/or electricity bills due from them, amount
paid and outstanding balance/s, if any, and other data that would facilitate
the proper monitoring of their compliance with the 2015 Market Code;
g. submit the list or records of delinquent stallholders accounts to the City
Accounting Office for proper recording in the books of accounts of the
City; and
h. henceforth, ensure that the provisions of the 2015 Market Code of General
Trias, particularly those related to the collection of market fees,
imposition of surcharges for late or non-payment of fees, cancellation of
the contracts of lease of stalls, sub-leasing or selling of privilege for
occupancy of stalls, leasing of two or more stalls to one person and
securing of business permit and license are strictly enforced, and that
collections and receivables from market stallholders are properly
accounted in the books of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to provide the information
required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to facilitate the auditorial
review and technical evaluation of the contract;
b) City General Services Officer (CGSO) to certify the IARs that the services
had been rendered and require the Contractor to submit certification
and/or tickets from the sanitary landfill as proof that all waste collected
had been disposed of or delivered to a sanitary landfill or other property
facility, as permitted or approved by the Department of Environment and
Natural Resources; and
c) City Accountant to submit the lacking documents necessary to support
the relevant disbursements made, and henceforth, refrain from
processing claims with incomplete supporting documents or information,
to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) City Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the Office Orders or any
equivalent document from the City Mayor showing that the
personnel/workers who claimed COVID-19 hazard pay were authorized to
report for work during the ECQ and Modified ECQ; and
b) City Budget Officer and City Accountant to properly account the COVID-19
hazard pay of COS and JO workers to Maintenance and Other Operating
Expenses to reflect in the Statement of Financial Performance the correct
expense incurred in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant, City Disaster
Risk Reduction Management Officer (CDRRMO) and City Planning and
Development Coordinator (CPDC) to submit to the Audit Team the LDRRMC’s
approved LDRRMFIP and supplemental investment program approved by the
Sangguniang Panlungsod covering all COVID-19 related programs, projects,
and activities to avoid suspension of the transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) City Accountant to properly record the purchases of welfare goods under
the account Welfare Goods for Distribution and the distributed portion to
Welfare Goods Expenses. He should also maintain Supplies Ledger Cards
for all welfare goods; and
b) City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer/City General Services
Officer to maintain Stock Cards to record the purchases and issuances of
welfare goods. The issuances of these items should be supported with
the Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) and reported monthly to the City
Accounting Office through the preparation and submission of monthly
Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) as basis of the City
Accountant in recording issued items under the account Welfare Goods
Expenses.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) BAC Secretariat to submit to the Audit Team justifications on its inability to
comply with the posting requirement under GPPB Circular No. 01-2020, for
further evaluation; and
b) BAC Secretariat and the City Accountant to submit to the Audit Team the
Mayor’s or Business Permit, Income/Business Tax Returns and Omnibus
Sworn Statement (OSS) of the suppliers pertaining to COVID-19
expenditures totaling P198,523,269.10, to avoid issuance of audit
suspension.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the responsible officials to
immediately resolve the cause of delays and expedite the implementation of
the projects of the 20 percent Development Fund.

We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Development
Council to reprogram the funds allotted for project that have been
implemented by the DPWH and any unexpended balance of completed
projects with the concurrence of the Sangguniang Panlungsod, for other
development projects that will address the present needs of the City and its
constituents.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the CGSO to:
a) prepare the Property Inventory Form and submit the same to the Audit
Team and GIF, GSIS not later than April 30 of each year; and
b) apply for insurance with the GSIS to cover all insurable properties of the
City in compliance with the provisions of RA No. 656, as amended by PD
No. 245 and Section 5.1 of COA Circular No. 2018-002 dated May 31, 2018.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned Property officials
to prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee a duly
accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to
facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the
unserviceable property, after which, ensure their immediate disposal to
prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.

We recommended that the City Mayor reinforce to the City Environment and
Natural Resources Officer and all those in-charge of garbage collection of
their mandated duties to implement RA No. 9003 and Municipal Ordinance
No. 13-03 and the possible administrative and criminal liabilities for their
continued disregard of the “no segregation/no collection policy.”

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GFPS-TWG, with the
assistance of the City Planning and Development Coordinator to complete the
establishment and maintenance of GAD Database containing statistics and
sex- disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to
serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy
formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Item 1,
Section 4.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE at least once a year and submit the RPCPPE classified per category/type to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and coordinate closely and seek assistance from the City Accounting Office for the classification of PPE according to category and in reconciling the results of the RPCPPE with the accountability records;
b) City Accounting Office (CAO) to continue its efforts to determine the details of the PPE amounting to P489,524,727.30; and
c) CGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein the relevant information for proper monitoring.
observations.managementAction: Continuous reconciliation of PPE prior to 2013 records are being undertaken by the Office of General Accounting. The CAO continuously exerted efforts in determining the details of the PPE. Started to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating only identifiable and recovered documents.
observations.reason: Inadequate records to the acquisition of PPE prior to 2013 hinders the reconciliation of records and inventory. Difficulty of retrieval of documents. Difficulty of retrieval of old records.

observations.managementAction: a) review existing Contracts of Lease and/or facilitate the execution of formal agreements with the market stallholders making sure that the said contracts are prepared in accordance with the 2015 Market Code of General Trias; Demand letters to delinquent stallholders to collect the market stall rental fees and electricity bills have been issued; Reconciliation of records have been conducted and investigation has been made by the concerned offices; Lessee of stall no.147 had been paying the stall rentals; Lessees with two or more stalls were identified and addressed; Records of individual market stallholders updated; list of delinquent stallholders to be submitted to City Accounting Office; Contracts of Lease to be prepared in accordance with the 2015 Market Code.
observations.reason: Not Due to pandemic the preparation of Contracts of Lease was not facilitated. Due to pandemic the City was not able to collect market rentals from the stallholders. Lack of coordination of the concerned offices. Inability of the City Treasurer to submit the list of delinquent stallholders. Inability of the City to prepare Contract of Lease in accordance with the 2015 Market Code. Reiterated in audit observation no. 2, part 2 of this report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to provide the information required under COA Circular No. 2012-001 to facilitate the auditorial review and technical evaluation of the contract;
b) City Environment and Natural Resources Officer to take steps to improve the monitoring system on the contractor’s performance and ensure compliance with the terms and obligations; and
c) City Accountant to submit the lacking documents necessary to support the relevant disbursements made, and henceforth, refrain from processing claims with incomplete supporting documents or information, to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances thereon.
observations.managementAction: The BAC and the CGSO had already submitted the required information and documents stated in letters a to d and certification from the sanitary landfill that the waste collected had been disposed of, respectively, on April 19, 2021. Moreover, the General Services Officer and the City Accountant agreed to comply with the audit recommendations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) strictly implement the provisions of Item 5.0 of DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and ensure that the implementation of the 20 per cent Development Fund is in accordance with the timeline indicated in the City’s Annual Investment Plan (AIP), so that timely and maximum benefits are enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries;
b) require the City Development Council and City Planning and Development Coordinator to properly plan the programs/projects of the 20 per cent Development Fund so as to avoid realignments and delays in their implementation; and
c) require the City Engineering Office (CEO) to ensure that detailed engineering such as survey and site inspection/investigation are completely conducted before the preparation of the plans to avoid delays in the implementation of projects.
observations.managementAction: Detailed timelines and schedules have been incorporated in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP), however, due to pandemic implementation of some projects had been delayed. A careful planning of the projects/programs of the 20 per cent Development Fund has been conducted by the concerned offices as no realignment has been made for the year. The City Engineering Office complied with the recommendation to avoid delays of the projects.
observations.reason: There were still problems encountered during the implementation of projects. The 20 percent Development Fund projects were scheduled to be implemented by the first semester of 2021, but the present situation of the COVID-19 pandemic might again cause delays on newly-started or on-going infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Development Council (CDC) and City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to use the General Fund instead of the 20 per cent Development Fund in implementing the City Hall Site Development project and henceforth, ensure that only projects that contribute to social, economic and environmental development are included in the Annual Investment Plan for the 20 per cent Development Fund, pursuant to the DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.
observations.managementAction: No expenditures/disbursements have been made for the City Hall Site Development Project and exclusion from the Annual Investment Plan of the 20 per cent Development Fund has been considered. The General Fund will be used for this project once being pursued.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC)-City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer (CDRRMO) and the CPDC to:
a) provide accurate timetables or schedules of implementation of all the projects and activities in the Local Disaster Risk Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation; and
b) review the programs with unexpended balances and determine whether these are still feasible, otherwise, re-program the funds allotted for such projects by submitting a revised plan upon the recommendation of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and approval by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor.
observations.managementAction: A thorough review has been made on the programs with unexpended balances and revision has been made as recommended by LDRRM Council and approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor. The LCE has directed and sent communication letter to all concerned departments for proper utilization of LDRRMFIP including the timetables/schedule of implementation of projects and activities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials to immediately resolve the boundary dispute to expedite the implementation of the General Trias City Park project funded by the National Government.
observations.managementAction: Boundary dispute has been settled and project has been completed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to exert efforts to locate old GSIS files and/or request from the GSIS for records or certification of arrears or no-arrears, for the years 2008 and earlier, to facilitate the remittance or adjustments in the books of the amount of P340,792.77.
observations.managementAction: Adjustments in the book of accounts have already been made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned Property officials to prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee a duly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the unserviceable property, after which, ensure their immediate disposal to prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.
observations.managementAction: Partial Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property has been made by the concerned department.
observations.reason: Non-submission of the report to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor as the Local Chief Executive ensure that the provisions of RA No. 9003 and relevant ordinance on solid waste management are strictly enforced in the City. We also recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod revisit and review Municipal Ordinance (MO) No. 13-03 for possible updating, revision, amendment or enactment of a new ordinance consistent with RA No. 9003. We further recommended that the City Mayor reinforce to the City Environment and Natural Resources Officer and all those in-charge of garbage collection of their mandated duties to implement RA No. 9003 and Municipal Ordinance No. 13-03 and the possible administrative and criminal liabilities for their continued disregard of the "no segregation/no collection policy."
observations.managementAction: The City Environment and Natural Resources Officer commented that the City had started the implementation of various programs on recyclable waste collection and diversion in partnership with Plastic Bank Philippines, junkshops and Barangay MRFs/MRS. Also, for 2021, they will focus on biodegradable waste management and diversion such as additional acquisition of biogas digester, rapid composting machine and promotion of urban and community gardening with composting component to different barangays. They also promised to intensify IEC campaign on solid waste segregation at source and at household level and strictly implement the “no segregation/no collection policy.”
observations.reason: Revisited and MO No. 13-10 reviewed. The no segregation/no collection policy was still not implemented.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the GFPS-TWG, with the assistance of the City Planning and Development Coordinator to establish and maintain the GAD Database containing statistics and sex-disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Item 1, Section 4.B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: The City Planning and Development Coordinator and GFPS-TWG explained that the CBMS was already underway, however, due to COVID-19 pandemic, its implementation based on the set timeline had been delayed. However, as of April 20, 2021 the CBMS was almost 90 percent accomplished and as soon as the CBMS and Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) survey was completed the City could generate sex-disaggregated data and gender statistics needed for their GAD database.
observations.reason: GAD Database was not yet completed. Due to COVID-19 pandemic the CBMS implementation was delayed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor issue an Executive Order (EO) to establish a GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System as required under Sections 3.4, 5.1 and 5.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction: EO was issued by the LCE for establishing GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated that the City Mayor direct the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS)‑Technical Working Group (TWG), with the assistance of the City Planning and Development Coordinator, to establish and maintain a GAD Database containing statistics and sex‑disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender‑responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Item 1, Section 4.B of PCW‑DILG‑DBM‑NEDA JMC No. 2013‑01. We also recommended that the City continue to capacitate the personnel involved in the GAD Program by sending them to attend relevant trainings and seminars for capacity development.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor commented that Community‑Based Monitoring System (CBMS) data seem to be the best source of the updated sex‑disaggregated data for GAD database. The implementation of CBMS started in September 2019 but due to non‑availability of DILG Provincial Trainer’s, the project was implemented two months delayed. The Sangguniang Panlungsod (SP) enacted SP Resolution No. 03‑2019‑153 dated September 30, 2019 authorizing the Mayor to enter into Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) with CBMS Network Team and DILG for the new version of CBMS called CBMS Accelerated Poverty Profiling (APP). The APP is the latest CBMS Program that will be adopted by the City and considered as major reason why CBMS implementation was delayed in 2019, that affected the establishment of an updated GAD database using CBMS results. Furthermore, the concerned offices believed that the data encoding of the recently concluded Quarantine Alleviation Program (QAP) shall be utilized after its completion using Electronic (E)‑QAP Listing Card Android App aimed to be finished by July 2020. The data to be generated in this undertaking would be crucial in the updating of the GAD Database of the City. Furthermore, the concerned offices believed that the data encoding of the recently concluded Quarantine Alleviation Program (QAP) shall be utilized after its completion using Electronic (E)‑QAP Listing Card Android App aimed to be finished by July 2020. The data to be generated in this undertaking would be crucial in the updating of the GAD Database of the City. The personnel involved in the GAD Program were sent to attend relevant trainings and seminars for capacity development.
observations.reason: The Annual GAD Plan and Budget was still based on the Community‑Based Monitoring System (CBMS) using year 2015 data. CBMS implementation was delayed due to non‑availability of DILG Provincial Trainer’s and transition to CBMS APP (CBMS Accelerated Poverty Profiling).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE
at least once a year and submit the RPCPPE classified per category/type
to the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and coordinate
closely with the City Accounting Office in reconciling the results of the
RPCPPE with the accountability records;
b) City Accounting Office to continue its efforts to determine the detail of the
PPE amounting to P489,524,727.30; and
c) CGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein the relevant
information for proper monitoring.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and Market
Supervisor to:
a) review existing Contracts of Lease and/or facilitate the execution of
formal agreements with market stallholders who have no contracts yet
with the City, making sure that the contracts are prepared in accordance
with the 2015 Market Code of General Trias;
b) send demand letters to delinquent stallholders to collect the market stall
rental fees and electricity bills totaling P4,881,882.66 plus the
corresponding surcharges, with the reminder that their failure to settle
their accounts within 30 days from due date is a sufficient ground for the
automatic cancellation of the contract of lease;
c) require all market stallholders to secure Mayor’s or business permits and
license, otherwise, impose upon them the sanctions or penalties provided
under relevant City ordinance/s;
d) investigate and explain the discrepancies between the CTO and BPLO
records, specifically on the 39 stallholders who secured business permits
from the BPLO, but whose names did not appear in the record of the CTO
as stall lessees, and on stall number 147, which appeared to be vacant per
CTO’s records, but a business permit was shown to have been issued to a
lessee by the BPLO;
e) investigate and explain why seven business owners with permits were
allowed to lease two or more market stalls which were not located in the
same section of the market, in violation of Section 16 of the 2015 Market
Code of General Trias;
f) update and complete the records of individual market stallholders to
include relevant information such as their names, residential addresses,
contact numbers, business permits and license numbers and issuance
dates, market rental fees and/or electricity bills due from them, amount
paid and outstanding balance/s, if there is/are any, and other data that
would facilitate the proper monitoring of their compliance with the 2015
Market Code;
g) submit the list or records of delinquent stallholders accounts to the City
Accounting Office for proper recording in the books of accounts of the
City; and henceforth,
h) ensure that the provisions of the 2015 Market Code of General Trias,
particularly those related to the collection of market fees, imposition of
surcharges for late or non-payment of fees, cancellation of the contracts
of lease of stalls, sub-leasing or selling of privilege for occupancy of
stalls, leasing of two or more stalls to one person and securing of
business permit and license are strictly enforced, and that collections and
receivables from market stallholders are properly accounted in the books
of the City.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee to submit the necessary supporting
documents to the Contract, and the supplemental/extension contract in
excess of the 5,042 truckloads of garbage, together with a detailed
breakdown of the ABC and the contract amount showing in sufficient
detail the derivation thereof;
b) City Environment and Natural Resources Officer to take steps to improve
the monitoring system on the contractor’s performance and compliance
with the terms and obligations stipulated in the contract; and
c) City Accountant to submit the lacking documents necessary to support
the relevant disbursements made, and henceforth, refrain from
processing claims with incomplete supporting documents, to avoid audit
suspensions/disallowances thereon.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) strictly implement the provisions of Item 5.0 of DILG and DBM JMC
No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 and ensure that the implementation of
the 20 per cent Development Fund is in accordance with the timeline
indicated in the City’s Annual Investment Plan (AIP) so that timely and
maximum benefits are enjoyed by the intended beneficiaries;
b) require the City Development Council and City Planning and Development
Coordinator to properly plan the programs/projects of the 20 per cent
Development Fund so as to avoid realignments and delays in their
implementation; and
c) require the CEO to ensure that detailed engineering such as survey and
site inspection/investigation are completely conducted before the
preparation of the plans to avoid del ays in the implementation of projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Development Council
and City Planning and Development Coordinator to use the General Fund
instead of the 20 per cent Development Fund in implementing the City Hall
Site Development project, and henceforth, ensure that only projects that
contribute to social, economic and environmental development are included
in the Annual Investment Plan for the 20 per cent Development Fund,
pursuant to DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-CDRRMO and the CPDC
to:
a) provide accurate information on the timetables or schedules of
implementation of all the projects and activities in the LDRRMFIP for
proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation; and
b) review the programs with unexpended balances and determine whether
these are still feasible, otherwise, re-program the funds allotted for such
projects by submitting a revised plan upon the recommendation of the
Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and approval by
the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials to
immediately resolve the boundary dispute to expedite the implementation of
the General Trias City Park project funded by the National Government.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to exert
efforts to locate old GSIS files and/or request from the GSIS for records or
certification of arrears or no-arrears, for the years 2008 and earlier, to facilitate
the remittance or adjustments in the books of the amount of P340,792.77.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned Property officials to
prepare and submit to the Disposal/Appraisal Committee a duly
accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property to
facilitate the determination of the appraised value and mode of disposal of the
unserviceable property, after which, ensure their immediate disposal to
prevent further deterioration and reduction of their economic value.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Environment and Natural
Resources Officer and other concerned City officials to extend the necessary
support to the remaining 23 non-compliant barangays to enable them to
establish their respective MRF.

We also recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod revisit and review
Municipal Ordinance No. 13-03 to for possible updating, revision, amendment
or enactment of a new ordinance to ensure that the provisions therein are
consistent with the solid waste management framework and principles
enunciated under RA No. 9003. We further recommended that the City Mayor
as Local Chief Executive ensure that the provisions of RA No. 9003 and
relevant ordinance on solid waste management are strictly enforced in the
City.

We reiterated our recommendation that the City Mayor direct the GFPS-TWG,
with the assistance of the City Planning and Development Coordinator, to
establish and maintain a GAD Database containing statistics and sex-
disaggregated data systematically gathered and regularly updated to serve as
basis for gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation
and subjected to gender analysis, in compliance with Item 1, Section 4.B of
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor issue an Executive Order (EO) to establish a
GAD Monitoring and Evaluation System as required under Sections 3.4, 5.1 and
5.2 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE at least once a year and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year; and coordinate closely with the City Accounting Office in reconciling the results of the RPCPPE with the accountability records;
b) CAO to continue its efforts to determine the detail of the PPE amounting to P489,524,727.30; and
c) CGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein the relevant information for proper monitoring.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office has difficulty in retrieving records particularly the cost of the PPE in the previous years. The Accounting personnel are now working backward as the recent acquired PPE has already been properly accounted and recorded. Likewise, the CGSO has started to maintain PCs per category of PPE. Moreover, the City Mayor directed the Inventory Committee, City Accounting Office and City General Services Office to work hand on hand in the maintenance of the PPE records and to comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Inability of the Inventory Committee to complete the conduct of physical count of all PPE. Reiterated in audit observation no. 1 Difficulty in the retrieval of documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a) City Accountant to adopt the Perpetual Inventory System for the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials as prescribed under PPSAS 12 and Sections 4(j) and 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
b) City General Services Officer (CGSO) to submit regularly the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) together with the Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) to the Accounting Office to serve as its bases in accounting for the issuances of supplies and materials.
observations.managementAction: The City Accounting Office had already adopted the Perpetual Inventory System. The City General Services Officer submitted the SSMI together with the original copies of the Requisition Issue Slip (RIS) to the Accounting Office to serve as its bases in accounting for the issuances of supplies and materials.
observations.reason: Difficulty in the retrieval of documents.

observations.managementAction: The City Mayor and the concerned officials agreed compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason:
a) Inability to include the information as part of the contract.
b) The assigned personnel to monitor the garbage collection have no monitoring records/logbooks to facilitate the evaluation and validation of the correctness of the Service Invoices (SIs) submitted by the Contractor, or to prove that there was actual monitoring undertaken of such activities by the City Government.
c) There were still claims with incomplete documentation and compliance in the ensuing year.
d) The City Budget Officer and the City Accountant had already properly account the expenses for the collection, hauling and disposal of solid waste to Environment/Sanitary Services account to reflect in the Statement of Financial Performance the correct expenses incurred in the ensuing year.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a) address the lack of personnel in the Engineering Office and proper planning, and
a) require the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to incorporate in the 20 per cent DF Annual Investment Plan the detailed schedules/timelines in the implementation of the programs/activities/projects for the proper guidance of all concerned personnel;
b) require the City Engineering Office (CEO) to ensure that detailed engineering such as survey and site inspection/investigation are completely conducted before the preparation of the plans to avoid delays in the implementation of projects; and
c) require the City Development Council (CDC) and CPDC to review the projects for reprogramming and to determine whether these are still feasible and priority projects, otherwise reprogram the funds allotted for such projects including any unexpended balance of completed projects with the concurrence of the Sangguniang Panlungsod, for other development projects that will address the present needs of the City and its constituents, and ensure that corresponding timelines for the reprogrammed/new projects are set.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor stated that the programs, projects and activities identified in the current 20 per cent Development Fund were not implemented on time due to lack of engineering personnel who can focus on the project planning, monitoring and actual implementation. However, he instructed the CEO and City Planning and Development Coordinator to comply with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Lack of personnel in the Engineering Office; Problems like Right-of-Way, project location/sites have not been identified, the location is a private property, the Department of Public Works and Highways had already implemented the project and program of works were not yet prepared by the CEO.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Officer-in-Charge (OIC) City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (CDRRMO) and the City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to:
a) provide timetables or schedules of implementation of all the projects and activities in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP) for proper guidance and monitoring of their implementation; and
b) review the programs with unexpended balances and determine whether these are still feasible, otherwise, re-program the funds allotted for such projects by submitting a revised plan upon the recommendation of the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and approval by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor and the concerned officials agreed compliance with the audit recommendation. The previous years’ unexpended balances were already realigned to other mitigation and preparedness projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to trace the source agencies of the subject unutilized funds amounting to P978,803.49 and immediately remit the same to the identified Source Agencies as required under Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94‑013 dated December 13, 1994.
observations.managementAction: The unutilized balances of the funds received from various NGAs were already remitted by the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the responsible focal persons to expedite the implementation of the projects/programs funded by the various NGAs.
observations.managementAction: Projects funded by the various NGAs were already implemented by the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, the School Division Superintendent, City Budget Officer and City Accountant see to it that the Special Education Fund is utilized for purposes provided under Section 4 of DepEd‑DBM‑DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor and the School Division Superintendent, City Budget Officer and City Accountant ensured that the Special Education Fund is utilized in accordance with Section 4 of DepEd‑DBM‑DILGJC No. 1, s. 2017.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a) Office of the Vice Mayor and Sangguniang Panlungsod to submit documents showing the purpose of the meetings, fora, surveys, consultations, and attendance of participants and minutes of meetings to give reasonable assurance that they are responsive to the mandate of the Office to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit; and
b) City Accountant to ensure that all claims are proper, incurred in the performance of official duties, and adequately documented before they are processed for payment.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the Vice Mayor had already submitted the supporting documents showing the purpose. The Office of the Vice Mayor tried their best to plan meetings and conferences. The City Accountant assured that all claims are proper and adequately documented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Environment and Natural Resources Officer and other concerned City officials to extend the necessary support to the remaining 23 non‑compliant barangays to enable them to establish their respective Material Recovery Facility (MRF). We also recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod revisit and review Municipal Ordinance No. 13‑03 for possible updating, revision, amendment or enactment of a new ordinance to ensure that the provisions therein are consistent with the solid waste management framework and principles enunciated under RA No. 9003. We further recommended that the City Mayor ensure that the provisions of RA No. 9003 and relevant ordinance on solid waste management are strictly enforced in the City.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor instructed the concerned officials to give priority on the compliance with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Lack of MRF in the 23 barangays. Reiterated in audit observation no. 10. Not to be complied in CY 2020.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to exert more effort in retrieving pertinent records of old PPEs, one way is by seeking assistance of the Accounting Office. Moreover, require the Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties acquired under the Special Education Fund and prepare the RPCPPE to comply with Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The Accounting Office and the GSO be instructed to maintain an updated and complete PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, to reconcile the balances of the accounts, in compliance with Section 375(d) of RA No. 7160.
observations.reason: Difficulty in retrieval of documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all PPE at least once a year
and submit the RPCPPE not later than January 31 of each year; and
coordinate closely with the City Accounting Office (CAO) in reconciling the
results of the RPCPPE with the accountability records;
b) CAO to continue its efforts to determine the details of the PPE amounting to
P489,524,727.30; and
c) CGSO to maintain PCs per category of PPE indicating therein the relevant
information for proper monitoring.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the:
a) City Accountant to adopt the Perpetual Inventory System for the receipt and
issuance of supplies and materials as prescribed under PPSAS 12 and
Sections 4(j) and 121 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and
b) City General Services Officer to submit regularly the SSMI together with the
original copies of the RIS to the Accounting Office to serve as its bases in
accounting for the issuances of supplies and materials.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Secretariat to submit the necessary
supporting documents to the contract, together with a detailed breakdown of
the ABC and the contract amount showing sufficient detail the derivation
thereof such as the number of paleros/laborers’ crew, prices of spare parts,
machine shop cost and other maintenance parts used;
b) City Environment and Natural Resources Officer to take steps to improve the
monitoring system on the contractor’s performance and compliance to the
terms and obligations stipulated in the contract;
c) City Accountant to submit the lacking documents necessary to support the
relevant disbursements made and henceforth, refrain from processing claims
with incomplete supporting documents to avoid audit suspensions/disallowances
thereon; and
d) City Budget Officer and the City Accountant to properly account the
expenses for the collection, hauling and disposal of solid waste to
Environment/Sanitary Services account to reflect in the Statement of
Financial Performance the correct expenses incurred in the ensuing year.

We recommended that the City Mayor address the lack of personnel in the
Engineering Office and proper planning, and direct the:
a) City Planning and Development Coordinator (CPDC) to incorporate in the 20
percent DF Annual Investment Plan the detailed schedules/timelines in the
implementation of the programs/activities/project for the proper guidance of
all concerned personnel;
b) CEO to ensure that detailed engineering such as survey and site
inspection/investigation are carried out sufficiently before the preparation of
the plans to avoid delays in the implementation of projects; and
c) City Development Council (CDC) and CPDC to review the projects
enumerated for funding by the continuing appropriation and determine
whether these are still feasible and should be priority projects of the City.
Otherwise, reprogram the funds allotted for such projects including any
unexpended balance of completed projects with the concurrence of the
Sangguniang Panlungsod, for other development projects that will address
the present needs of the City and its constituents, and ensure that
corresponding timelines for the reprogrammed/new projects are set.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the OIC-CDRRMO and the CPDC to:
a) provide information timetables or schedules of implementation of all the
projects and activities in the LDRRMFIP for proper guidance and monitoring
of their implementation; and
b) review the programs of the unexpended balances and determine whether
these are still feasible, otherwise, reprogram the funds allotted for such
projects by submitting a revised plan upon the recommendation of the Local
Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and approval by the
Sangguniang Panlungsod and the City Mayor.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant and the City
Treasurer to trace the source agencies of the subject unutilized funds amounting
to P978,803.49 and immediately remit the same to the identified Source Agencies
as required under Items 4.9 and 6.7 of COA Circular No. 94-013 dated December
13, 1994.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the responsible focal persons to
expedite the implementation of the projects/programs funded by the various
National Government Agencies.

We recommended that the City Mayor submit justification or explanation for the
charges of the above expenses against the Special Education Fund.

We also recommended that henceforth, the City Mayor, the School Division
Superintendent, City Budget Officer and Accountant see to it that the Special
Education Fund is utilized for purposes provided under Section 4 of DepEd-DBM-
DILG JC No. 1, s. 2017 to ensure that the purpose for which the fund was
intended is met.

We recommended that the City Vice Mayor and Sangguniang Panlungsod
Secretary to:
a) submit documents supporting/showing the purpose of the meetings, fora,
surveys, and consultations, and attendance of participants and minutes of
meetings to give reasonable assurance that they are responsive to the
mandate of the Office to avoid suspension/disallowance in audit; and
b) adequately plan meetings and conferences.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City Accountant to see to it
that all claims are proper, incurred in the performance of official duties, and
adequately documented before they are processed for payment.

We recommended that the City Mayor provide the necessary support in enjoining
the remaining 23 barangays to establish MRF and strictly implement waste
segregation at source and comply with all other prescribed procedures and
guidelines under RA No. 9003.

We also recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod enact ordinance
intensifying and enforcing mandatory segregation of waste at source and “no
segregation/no collection” rule to avoid the incurrence of additional cost for the
collection, hauling and disposal of garbage.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the GFPS-TWG, with the assistance of
the City Planning and Development Coordinator, to establish and maintain a GAD
Database containing statistics and sex disaggregated data systematically
gathered and regularly updated to serve as basis for gender-responsive planning,
programming and policy formulation and subjected to gender analysis, in
compliance with Section 4.B, Item 1 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of
PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA.

We also recommended that the City continue with its effort to capacitate the
personnel involved in the GAD Program by sending them to attend relevant
trainings and workshops.

Henceforth, copy of the Contract of Sale/Deed of Sale/Deed of Absolute Sale for lot acquisition and all the required supporting documents for related payments should be submitted to the Auditor within five days from perfection of the contract so that prompt contract and technical evaluation can be conducted.
observations.managementAction: The ownership of the two parcels of land totaling P19,291,600.00 for the construction of access road from Governor’s drive going to some portion of residential area and Gentri Medical Center and by-pass road traversing Barangay Manggahan and Biclatan were already transferred to the City and the required documents were submitted to the Auditor. Copy of the Contract of Sale/Deed of Sale/Deed of Absolute Sale for lot acquisition and all the required supporting documents for related payments were submitted to the Auditor.
observations.reason: The required documents were submitted to the Auditor.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit to the Audit Team the required eligibility documents cited in Sections 23.1 and 23.5.1 of the Revised IRR for review and evaluation. We also recommended that henceforth, the BAC observe the above provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods specifically on the Supplier’s eligibility requirements.
observations.managementAction: The BAC has already submitted the eligibility documents. The BAC has already observed the submission of the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the City Mayor and the concerned City officials and employees to ensure that payments for supplies and materials, as well as other operating expenses, be on reimbursement basis, made directly to the suppliers. Adopt the alternative mode of procurement and include all the goods to be purchased in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and properly plan the said procurement pursuant to Section 7 of RA No. 9184 to minimize reimbursement of procurement transactions.
observations.managementAction: Management has already minimized the payments on reimbursement basis. Adopt the alternative mode of procurement and include all the goods to be purchased in the APP and properly plan the said procurement pursuant to Section 7 of RA No. 9184 to minimize reimbursement of procurement transactions.
observations.reason: Expenses for emergency cases like meals for visitors and materials for activities were still made on reimbursement basis.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials of the City to submit all lacking documents of the subject transactions and instruct the: a. BAC, OIC-General Service Officer and the City Budget Officer that henceforth, all construction materials to be utilized be included in the APP; b. BAC and the GSO to consolidate all the requested materials to ensure that procurement be done through public bidding instead of small value procurement; and c. City Accountant to stop processing claims with incomplete/deficient documentations.
observations.managementAction: The Management already submitted the required documents. All construction materials were included in the APP. The BAC and GSO are trying their best to consolidate the requested materials. The City Accountant promised to stop processing claims with incomplete/deficient documentations.
observations.reason: Due to the urgency of the repair of various properties and facilities led the Management to implement procurement through small value procurement.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the City Mayor require the OIC-General Services Officer to submit all delivery documents on the procured supplies, materials and equipment regardless of the mode of procurement, that is, public bidding or alternative mode of procurement within 24 hours after delivery, in compliance with Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002, to ensure timely inspection of the delivered items. The City DRRMO be required to submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the Funds, certified by the Municipal Accountant to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation of the City’s utilization of the CDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The BAC has already submitted the notices of deliveries of supplies and materials to the Audit Team. The Management is already submitting the notices and the required documents will be included.
observations.reason: Some documents were not submitted within 24 hours after acceptance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer be required to enforce collection of the underpayment of business taxes, including the penalties and surcharges from the suppliers/contractors identified to have underdeclared their gross sales. Moreover, instruct the OIC-Business Permit and Licensing Officer (BPLO) to impose penalties to those suppliers that already declared their business closed but still engaging business with the City and some of its Barangays without permit.
observations.managementAction: The City Treasurer’s Office and the BPLO exerted efforts to collect the identified underpaid business taxes and they were able to collect the amount of P1,434,670.91. The collection of the remaining amount is ongoing.
observations.reason: Some of the businesses were already closed and the collection of the remaining amount is ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor address the lack of personnel in the Engineering Office and instruct the City Planning and Coordinating Development Officer to incorporate in the 20% Development Fund AIP the detailed schedule of implementation of the programs/activities/project, with complete timeframes to address the causes of the delays in the implementation of the duly funded projects.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor informed the Audit Team that the City already hired two Engineers in March 2019 to undertake the preparation of the corresponding program of works and other engineering activities. Moreover, he instructed the concerned officials to adhere with the audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Lack of personnel in the Engineering Office and absence of schedule of projects implementation resulted in low utilization.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with source NGAs to request for the use of the unexpended/unutilized balances of the funds to other City beneficial projects. Otherwise, remit/return the idle funds to the concerned NGAs, if no longer needed.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor instructed the concerned department heads to make representation with the NGAs and request for the utilization of the unexpended balances of the fund for other beneficial projects, if possible. In case the NGAs would not allow, he instructed the City Treasurer to immediately remit the same to the identified Source Agencies.
observations.reason: The Fund remained unutilized or unremitted as of December 31, 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City DRRMO and the other concerned implementing offices to maximize the utilization of both the current and prior years’ allocation for CDRRMFIP by seeing to it that the CDRRMFIP are properly prepared and are implemented as planned/scheduled. We also recommended that the City DRRMO be required to submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the Funds, certified by the Municipal Accountant to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation of the City’s utilization of the CDRRMF.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor instructed the OIC-CDRRMO and the CPDC to include schedules of implementation in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP and review the unexpended balances of the fund to maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF.
observations.reason: Not all projects had specific detailed schedules; monthly reports were not consistently prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require: a. the BAC to submit the necessary documents supporting the contract for immediate technical review and evaluation; b. the concerned City officials to submit the detailed breakdown of the ABC and the contract amount showing sufficient detail as derivation including the number of paleros/laborers’ crew, prices of spare parts, machine shop cost and other maintenance parts used in detailed breakdown of the contract amount; c. the concerned City officials to submit the lacking documents supporting the payments, and d. the City Accountant to refrain from processing claims with incomplete supporting documents, otherwise Notice of Suspensions will be issued.
observations.managementAction: The BAC submitted the necessary documents. The Management submitted the lacking documents for CY 2017 transactions. The City Accountant promised to stop processing claims with incomplete/deficient documentations.
observations.reason: Awaiting submission of some documents; some claims still had incomplete supporting documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure full compliance with the basic requirements of RA No. 9003 by seeing to it that the Sangguniang Panlungsod enact ordinances that will increase the awareness and cooperation of the constituents and the Barangays on proper solid waste management particularly on the mandatory waste segregation at source.
observations.managementAction: During the exit conference, the City Mayor assured to extend support in the establishment of MRF for all barangays and comply with the prescribed basic environmental regulations required under RA No. 9003. Moreover, the City Vice Mayor promised to convey the matter to the Sangguniang Panlungsod for the enactment of the ordinance for no segregation/no collection rule.
observations.reason: The City still fell short in becoming fully compliant with the basic environmental regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BUB funds received from National Government Agencies be utilized fully for the benefits of the intended beneficiaries and proper use thereof in accordance with DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum Circular No. 7.
observations.managementAction: BUB Funds were fully utilized by the City.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to the conduct of physical inventory of all properties and prepare the RPCPPE to comply with Section 124 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. Moreover, require the Committee to conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties acquired under the Special Education Fund and prepare the RPCPPE to comply with Section 124 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for Local Government Units (LGUs), Volume I. Also, we recommended that the Accounting Office and the GSO be instructed to maintain an updated and complete PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively, to reconcile the balances of the accounts, in compliance with Section 375(d) of Republic Act (RA) No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: The Inventory Committee commented that the physical count of all PPE under the Special Education Fund is still ongoing. They informed that the difficulty in retrieving old documents/records hindered the reconciliation of the RPCPPE against the accounting records.
observations.reason: The Inventory Committee still unable to complete conduct a complete inventory of all PPE due to difficulty of retrieving old records.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit to the Audit Team the required eligibility documents cited in Sections 23.1 and 23.5.1 of the Revised IRR for review and evaluation. We also recommended that henceforth, the BAC observe the above provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods specifically on the Supplier’s eligibility requirements.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor had already instructed the BAC to immediately submit the required eligibility documents to COA.
observations.reason: The BAC had not submitted the required eligibility documents to COA for many contracts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to
exert more effort in retrieving pertinent records of old PPEs, one way is by seeking
the assistance of the Accounting Office. Moreover, require the Committee to
conduct a complete physical inventory of all properties acquired under the Special
Education Fund and prepare the RPCPPE to comply with Section 124 of the
MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I. The Accounting Office and the GSO be instructed to
maintain an updated and complete PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards,
respectively, to reconcile the balances of the accounts, in compliance with Section
375(d) of RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned City Officials to
submit to COA the lacking documents supporting the payments for the purchased
four parcels of land in 2017 and work on the immediate transfer of ownership of
these properties to the City. Henceforth, copy of the Contract of Sale/Deed of
Absolute Sale for lot acquisition and all the required supporting documents for
related payments should be submitted to the Auditor within five days from
perfection of the contract so that prompt contract and technical evaluation can be
conducted.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the BAC to submit to the
Audit Team the required eligibility documents cited in Sections 23.1 and 23.5.1 of
the Revised IRR for review and evaluation.

We also recommended that henceforth, the BAC observe the above
provisions of RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR in the procurement of goods
specifically on the Supplier’s eligibility requirements.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the City Mayor and the
concerned City officials and employees ensure that payments for supplies and
materials, as well as other operating expenses, be made directly to the suppliers.
Adopt the alternative mode of procurement and include all the goods to be
purchased in the APP and properly plan the said procurement pursuant to Section 7
of RA No. 9184 to minimize reimbursement of procurement transactions.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the concerned officials of the
City to submit all the lacking documents of the subject transactions and instruct
the:
a. BAC, OIC-General Service Officer and the City Budget Officer that
henceforth, all construction materials to be utilized be included in the
APP;
b. BAC and the GSO to consolidate all the requested materials to ensure that
procurement be done through public bidding instead of small value
procurement; and
c. City Accountant to stop processing claims with incomplete/deficient
documentation.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the City Mayor
require the OIC-General Services Officer to submit all delivery documents on the
procured supplies, materials and equipment regardless of the mode of procurement,
that is, public bidding or alternative mode of procurement within 24 hours after
delivery, in compliance with Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002, to ensure
timely inspection of the delivered items.

We recommended that the City Treasurer be required to enforce collection
of the underpayment of business taxes, including penalties and surcharges from the
suppliers/contractors identified to have underdeclared their gross sales. Moreover,
instruct the OIC-Business Permit and Licensing Officer (BPLO) to impose penalties
to those suppliers that already declared their business closed but still engaging
business with the City and some of its Barangays without permit.

We recommended that the City Mayor address the lack of personnel in the
Engineering Office and instruct the City Planning and Coordinating Officer to
incorporate in the 20% Development Fund Annual Investment Plan the detailed
schedule implementation of the programs/activities/project, with complete
timeframes as to address the causes of the delays in the implementation of the duly
funded projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with source
National Government Agencies (NGAs) to request for the use of the
unexpended/unutilized balances of the funds to other City beneficial projects.
Otherwise, remit/return the idle funds to the concerned NGAs, if no longer needed.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the CPDO to prepare a
comprehensive CDRRMFIP with specific/detailed list of PPAs for both current and
continuing appropriations, in compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular
No. 2012-002.

We also recommended that the City Mayor direct the City DRRMO and the
other concerned implementing offices to maximize the utilization of both the current
and prior years’ fund allocation for CDRRM programs, projects and activities by
seeing to it that the CDRRMFIP are properly prepared and are implemented as
planned/scheduled. Moreover, the City DRRMO be required to submit the Monthly
Report on the Sources and Utilization of the Funds, certified by the Municipal
Accountant to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation of the City’s utilization of
the CDRRMF.

We recommended that the City Mayor require:
a. the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit the necessary
documents supporting the contract, for the immediate technical review
and evaluation;
b. the concerned City officials to submit the detailed breakdown of the ABC
and the contract amount showing sufficient detail as derivation including
the number of paleros/laborers’ crew, prices of spare parts, machine shop
cost and other maintenance parts used in detailed breakdown of the
contract amount;
c. the concerned City officials to submit the lacking documents supporting
the payments, and
d. the City Accountant to refrain from processing claims with incomplete
supporting documents, otherwise, Notice of Suspensions will be issued.

We recommended that the City Mayor ensure full compliance with the basic
requirements of RA No. 9003 or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 by seeing
to it that the local Sangguniang Panlungsod enact ordinances that will increase the
awareness and cooperation of the constituents and the Barangays on proper solid
waste management particularly on the mandatory waste segregation at source.

We recommended that the BUB funds received from National Government
Agencies be utilized fully for the benefits of the intended beneficiaries and proper
use thereof in accordance with DBM-DILG-DSWD-NAPC Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 7.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Treasurer to prepare and submit a certified list of the taxpayers with the amount of taxes due and collectible based on the RPT Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards, which will be the basis of the City Accountant in setting up the Receivables at the beginning of the year, to establish with certainty the reported RPT and SET Receivables as of year-end.
observations.managementAction: The installation of modern computer system is on-going to ensure integrity of the information being generated by Real Property Tax Account Register/Taxpayer’s Index Cards.
observations.reason: Due to voluminous/ increasing number of taxpayers, the printing of RPTAS and Index Cards were not made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant, the City Treasurer and the City Assessor to coordinate with the concerned National Government Agencies to facilitate titling of the land. We also recommended that the concerned City officials ensure that all land acquired are titled/registered in the name of the City. Comply with the requirement embodied under Section 2 of PD No. 1445 to safeguard government ownership of properties.
observations.managementAction: The difficulty in retrieving some records/documents hindered the registration and titling of land in the name of the agency.
observations.reason: The difficulty in retrieving some records/documents hindered the registration and titling of land in the name of the agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer and other concerned officials to coordinate with the LBP-Cavite City Branch to facilitate the immediate adoption of the automated payroll system to address the risks of loss and/or misappropriation of funds and for an efficient and economical agency operation.
observations.managementAction: The City was unable to limit payments through reimbursement.
observations.reason: The City was unable to limit payments through reimbursement.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendation that the City Mayor direct the concerned City officials and employees to ensure that payments for supplies and materials, as well as other operating expenses, be made directly to the suppliers. Limit payments through reimbursement. Reiterated in Audit Observation No. 4, Part II of this report.
observations.managementAction: The concerned City Officials reasoned out that there were expenses that cannot be predicted ahead such as meals served to visitors, immediate purchase of gasoline and materials for activities. Nevertheless, they committed to adhere to the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require that all government vehicles with green plate with the name of the City, and the application for their insurance with the GSIS to provide the government adequate protection against damage or loss. All motor vehicles be covered with said motor vehicle insurance. We likewise recommended that all vehicles be covered with Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment, in compliance with Section 489 of the GAAM, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor had already instructed the concerned City officials that the registration/titling of the 13 motor vehicles in the name of the agency is still on going due to difficulty of retrieving old records, thus transfer of ownership cannot be immediately undertaken. The City officials were able to prepare RPLC. All motor vehicles acquired were already covered with ARE.
observations.reason: The registration of the 13 motor vehicles in the name of the agency is still on going due to difficulty of retrieving old records, thus transfer of ownership cannot be immediately undertaken.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the BAC Secretariat to prepare the PPMPs and APP consistent with the approved annual budget, in accordance with Sections 7.1 and 7.2 of the RIRR of RA No. 9184 and to ensure that all goods necessary for City’s operations are acquired.
observations.managementAction: The BAC Secretariat had prepared the PPMPs and APP consistent with the approved Annual Budget.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineer to submit the basis for the computation of ABC and justification for non-compliance with the aforementioned DO for our further evaluation. Henceforth, strictly comply with the guidelines provided under DPWH DO No. 22, series of 2015.
observations.managementAction: The agency had complied with the DO No. 22, series of 2015.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Contractor refund amount of corresponding to the difference between the contract cost and the COA Allowable Cost, otherwise, a Notice of Disallowance will be issued. We also recommended that henceforth, the concerned City officials be guided by the established technical standards in preparing Program of Works/Estimates to ensure reasonableness of the contract costs.
observations.managementAction: The Contractor was unable to refund the amount; hence a Notice of Disallowance will be issued. The concerned City officials had already guided with the established technical standards in the preparation of Program of Works/Estimates.
observations.reason: The Contractor was unable to refund the amount; hence a Notice of Disallowance will be issued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the OIC-General Services Officer to submit all delivery documents on the procured supplies, materials and equipment regardless of the mode of procurement, that is, public bidding or alternative mode of procurement within 24 hours after delivery, in compliance with Section 6.9 of COA Circular No. 2009-002, to ensure timely inspection of the delivered items.
observations.managementAction: The City already adopted the process of control measure on receipt of goods through public bidding. Only Notice of Deliveries and Request for Inspections on alternative method of procurement of goods were received.
observations.reason: Only Notice of Deliveries and Request for Inspections were received for the procurement of goods through alternative mode of procurement; not all delivery documents for public bidding were submitted within 24 hours.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer be required to enforce the collection of the underpayment of business taxes, including penalties and surcharges from the Suppliers/Contractors identified to have underdeclared their gross sales. Moreover, instruct the City Accountant to submit to the BPLO the names of the Suppliers/Contractors who transacted business with the City, including the payments made to them in the preceding calendar year, to serve as basis for the computation of business taxes.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor had directed the City Treasurer to enforce the collection of the underpayment of business taxes, including penalties and surcharges. He also instructed the OIC-BPLO to impose penalties to those suppliers that already declared their business closed but still dealing business with the City and with the barangays without permit.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and other concerned City officials establish negotiating the control and monitoring measures such as the use of a fleet card that will set reasonable monthly limits for fuel consumption of all motor vehicles assigned in every department/office to avoid incurrence of excessive use of fuels. We also recommended that the City Mayor require the officials and employees assigned with government motor vehicles to submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption monthly, in compliance with COA Circular No. 77-61 and for Auditor’s review and evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The City had already in the process of control measure negotiating the use of fleet card as one of the process of control measure.
observations.reason: The use of fleet card as one control measure was not yet fully implemented; Monthly Reports of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption were still not prepared.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC conduct thorough post-qualification evaluation to ensure that the City transact/procure legitimate suppliers who are financially capable of carrying out the transactions, otherwise a Notice of Disallowance will be issued. We also recommended that henceforth, the BAC ensure that the winning bidder is financially capable of carrying out the transactions.
observations.managementAction: The BAC ensured that the City procure goods directly from financially capable and legitimate/reputable suppliers.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor make representation with source NGAs to request for the use of the unexpended/unutilized balances of the funds to other City beneficial projects. Otherwise, remit/return the idle funds to the concerned NGAs, if no longer needed.
observations.managementAction: The agency had utilized the total amount of P599,295.28 for Trust Fund purposes. The City will make representation with the source agencies to use remaining balances.
observations.reason: As of December 31, 2017, the amount of P2,750,250.00 for Trust Fund still remains unutilized.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor revise the composition of the members of the City School Board, in compliance with the requirements of Section 98(b)(2) of RA No. 7160, so that the annual budget is prepared and approved by the duly constituted members of the Local School Board.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2017, the City already complied with the required membership as provided for under Section 98(b)(2) of RA No. 7160.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor expedite and maximize the full implementation and maximum utilization of the 20% Development Fund for the development projects, in accordance with the guidelines set forth under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 to attain the desired socio-economic and environmental outcomes.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor commented that the timeline set for the implementation of the projects under the 20% Development Fund was not followed due to lack of personnel particularly in the Engineering Department. To address the problem, he promised to hire adequate personnel. He already directed the concerned City officials to monitor the utilization of the fund and give priority to the full implementation of the funded projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require full compliance with the basic requirements of RA No. 9003 or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000. The local Sangguniang Panlungsod should enact ordinances that will increase the awareness and cooperation of the constituents and the Barangays on proper solid waste management particularly on the mandatory waste segregation at source.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor had already directed the GFPS to coordinate with the City Planning Office in setting-up and maintaining GAD database. Designate GAD Focal Person among the members of the GFPS to lead in mainstreaming of gender in the City’s programs, projects and activities and adhere strictly to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 in the implementation of GAD activities to achieve objectives.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct the City DRRMO and other concerned implementing offices to fully utilize the fund and implement all budgeted projects, programs and activities. Further, require the City DRRMO to submit monthly to the Audit Team the Report on the Sources and Utilization of the Funds, certified by the City Accountant, for proper and timely evaluation.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor had already directed the CPDO to prepare a comprehensive CDRRMFIP with specific and detailed list of projects, programs and activities in compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002. He also instructed the City DRRMO to prepare and submit the Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the Funds, certified by the Municipal Accountant to facilitate proper monitoring and evaluation of the City’s utilization of the CDRRMF.
observations.reason: No actions were made by the City DRRMO; submission of Monthly Reports was not made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor ensure full compliance with the basic requirements of RA No. 9003 or the Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 by seeing to it that the local Sangguniang Panlungsod enact ordinances that will increase the awareness and cooperation of the constituents and the Barangays on proper solid waste management particularly on the mandatory waste segregation at source.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor commented that the City is committed to address the problem on solid waste management and assured taking immediate action on the matter.
observations.reason: The City was not able to fully comply and the compliance level remained very low at 18%.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Engineering and City Office to expedite the implementation of infrastructure projects funded out of the BUB Funds. We also recommended that the City Welfare and Development Officer and the City Agriculturist be instructed to submit the lacking documents for further review and evaluation of the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: The City Mayor promised to immediately take actions on the matter. The City Welfare and Development Officer and the City Development Officer already submitted the lacking documents.
observations.reason: Some BUB projects remain unimplemented due to lack of personnel and other issues; two projects funded under CY 2015 remained unimplemented.
